title: What Every Retail Stocktake Reveals About the Year's Actual Margin
linkedin:
Every June, retail businesses across Queensland and NSW count their stock.
The numbers go to the accountant. Most owners do not read the commercial story they contain.

The June 30 stocktake produces your actual gross margin for the year. Cost of goods sold, calculated from opening stock plus the year's purchases minus the closing count, divided by sales revenue. That number includes the full impact of every clearance line, end-of-season reduction, and promotional markdown made across the twelve months just closed.

There are two patterns the EOFY data surfaces regularly in retail. The first is sell-through: which categories moved at full price, which required repeated discounting to clear, and whether the clearance decisions recovered margin or destroyed it. Early clearance at a shallow discount often captures adequate return. Clearance that runs progressively deeper through the season can sell units at or below cost once multiple promotional layers compound.

The second is the inventory position entering July. Closing stock at June 30 is working capital committed to goods not yet sold. A business entering the new financial year carrying heavy prior-season stock must manage that inventory alongside new season arrivals, often compressing margin through discounting that becomes unavoidable when old and new compete for the same shelf space.

Both pictures are in the stocktake data right now. Retail owners who read them before finalising the FY27 buying plan make those decisions from the year's actual commercial truth, not from a general sense of how it went.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

facebook:
The June 30 stocktake is one of the quieter rituals of running a retail business. Everything counted, closing stock recorded, numbers sent to the accountant.

But the data it produces tells you something your monthly reports cannot: the actual gross margin impact of every clearance discount, end-of-season markdown, and promotional reduction across the full year. For most retail owners, that combined number is different from what the individual decisions felt like at the time.

What did your year's clearance activity actually cost in gross margin points? Worth knowing before the FY27 buying plan is locked in.

#AustralianBusiness #Retail
google:
If you run a retail store in Brisbane, Queensland, or New South Wales, the June 30 stocktake is about to produce the most commercially useful data of your year.

The closing stock count, combined with your year's purchasing records, gives you the actual cost of goods sold for the twelve months just closed. Divide that by total sales revenue and you have your real gross margin percentage for the year. That figure includes the full impact of every clearance line, end-of-season reduction, and promotional markdown across the trading year.

Most retail owners hand this figure to the accountant without using it to review the pricing and buying decisions it reflects.

Two questions the data answers well: which product categories sold through at adequate margin, and which required repeated discounting that quietly reduced the year's gross margin. The second question also informs the buying plan for FY27, because the categories that over-bought in FY26 are the ones most likely to need clearance activity again if quantities are not adjusted.

The inventory position at June 30 matters too. Heavy unsold stock entering July is working capital tied up in goods the business still needs to sell, often at reduced prices alongside new season arrivals.

ProfitPulse works with retail business owners in Brisbane and across the East Coast on the financial analysis that turns EOFY data into better decisions for the year ahead. Read the full piece on the blog.
